UP-NS Merger: Houston Rail Impact Map

UP's own operating plan (STB FD 36873, Doc. 311221) vs. FRA blocked crossing, traffic, and collision data for the 8-county Houston complex. Click any line to trace the ripple effect.
ⓘ Sources and method (click to expand) - prepared from federal filings and FRA safety data, July 2026
Train volumes: Union Pacific / Norfolk Southern merger application operating plan workpapers, STB Docket FD 36873, Document 311221 ("311221 Spreadsheet," Electronic Appendices E, M and T). "Today" = Base Plan (2024 base year); "After merger" = Growth Plan total daily train count, excluding locals. These are the applicants' own projections. A train traveling across several segments is counted on each segment.
Why trains/day figures differ: the line numbers on this map are from the applicants' operating plan model (Appendix T) and count UP/NS road trains only. They exclude local trains and yard moves (about 48 percent of Houston train activity per TxDOT) and exclude other railroads using the same tracks under trackage rights (BNSF, Amtrak, CPKC). Where available, popups also show Actual 2024 density from Appendix C of the same document, which counts all railroads: for example, Tower 87 to Tower 26 shows 34 to 41 plan trains but 85 actual trains/day. The plan numbers are shown on the lines because they are the applicants' merger commitment; the actual density shows the real baseline communities live with. Measured per-crossing counts come from City of Houston sensors (seven crossings) and Trainfo data held by the East End District (94 crossings, used in the May 2023 impact study).
Line geometry: USDOT Bureau of Transportation Statistics, North American Rail Network Lines (NTAD). Corridor endpoints were placed by matching UP's stated segment mileages onto the rail network; segments marked "approximate placement" differ from UP's stated mileage by more than 25% and are drawn on the correct route but with approximate endpoints.
Blocked crossings: FRA Blocked Crossing Incident Reports, public reports at UP crossings in Harris, Galveston, Fort Bend, Waller, Liberty, Chambers, Brazoria and Montgomery counties, 2022 through 2025. Reports are voluntary public submissions and understate actual blockage.
Crossing inventory (traffic, trains, gates): FRA Grade Crossing Inventory System (Form 71), current records, accessed July 13, 2026. AADT = annual average daily highway traffic at the crossing as reported on the inventory record (reporting year varies by crossing). Trains/day at a crossing = railroad-reported daylight plus nighttime through trains plus switching movements.
Collisions: FRA Highway-Rail Grade Crossing Accident/Incident data (Form 57), January 2016 through June 2026, at crossings in the same 8 counties, matched to UP inventory records.
Junction history: Interlocking tower locations cross-checked against Texas Railroad History (txrrhistory.com) records for Towers 26, 30, 85, 86, 87 and 210.
Schools and fire stations: Public schools from NCES EDGE geocoded school file, school year 2024-25, all 8 counties. Fire stations from Harris County GIS (all agencies, Harris County only; stations in the other 7 counties are not yet mapped). Facilities shown are within 1 mile (schools) or 1.5 miles (fire stations) of a mapped UP corridor. Distances are straight-line.
Detour burden: distance from each at-grade crossing to the nearest open grade-separated crossing (bridge or underpass) on the UP network in the 8 counties, straight-line, from the same FRA inventory. Actual driving detours are longer.
Comment generator: the "Copy STB comment" buttons assemble the cited figures for that crossing or segment into a paragraph you can paste into a filing at stb.gov (Docket No. FD 36873) or a letter to officials. Figures come only from the sources above.
Time occupied estimate: daily through trains multiplied by average minutes a train occupies a crossing. City of Houston smart crossing sensors (Rail Safety Task Force monthly reports, 2023 through 2026) measure average occupancy of roughly 5 to 22 minutes per train at monitored East End crossings; monitored crossings use their measured occupancy minutes and measured daily occupancy counts (Dec 2025 and Feb 2026 reports) and all others use railroad-reported through trains and 11 minutes, the approximate sensor average. Stopped-train blockages are additional. The seven monitored crossings are Commerce St, Hirsch Rd, Leeland/Cullen, Lockwood St, Market St, Polk St and York St.
Monitored and funded crossings (stars): City of Houston sensor sites and grade separation funding from Rail Safety Task Force presentations: $36.9 million FRA award for the West Belt Grade Separation program (Commerce St and York St underpasses, Navigation Blvd underpass reconstruction); $40 million TxDOT off-system award for the Griggs / Long / Mykawa project; $10 million Broadway Pedestrian Bridge; $2 million East End Train Triangle study (Harris County Precinct 2 and City of Houston); Lockwood underpass proposed through the METRO grade separation effort; Leeland/Cullen feasibility study through TIRZ.
Emergency response evidence: FRA-sponsored research (Trainfo, "Assessing the Safety Benefits of a Real-Time Railroad Crossing Information System for Emergency Responders," DOT/FRA/ORD-25/02, January 2025) modeled and measured first responder delays at seven Houston crossings including Hirsch Rd, Commerce St, Leeland/Cullen, Lockwood, Market, Polk and York, finding responders impacted at each on a weekly basis. Grade separation candidates and costs for the East Belt (Canal $11M, Harrisburg $14M, Hirsch $6.1M, Lyons $6.7M, Wallisville $8.2M) are from the TxDOT Houston Region Freight Study (2021, with HNTB). The 2024 Texas Rail Plan (Appendix A, Table A-7) lists average trains per day as "Unknown" for every UP Houston Service Unit subdivision, meaning the state itself could not obtain or confirm train counts from the railroad.
High Injury Network: City of Houston Vision Zero High Injury Network (2022), the streets where most serious crashes concentrate. City of Houston only.
Business impact: East End District Impact Study (May 2023), using Trainfo sensor data at 94 East End crossings and Data Axle records for over 6,000 businesses: each additional railroad crossing within a half mile of a business is associated with a 3.43 percent decrease in sales, and each additional hour of average daily vehicle delay with a 45.4 percent decrease; over 65 percent of surveyed patrons reported train delays. eastenddistrict.com.
Regional context: TxDOT Houston Region Freight Study: about 1,200 roadway-rail crossings in the 8-county region carry almost five million vehicles daily; the 20-year public cost of crossing delay is estimated above $2.6 billion ($2.3 billion lost time, $146 million collisions, $191 million emissions and wasted fuel). Almost half (48 percent) of trains in the Houston network are local trains and yard engines, which are excluded from the merger workpaper counts shown on this map, so mapped trains/day understate total crossing activity. The study found a Dayton to Cleveland bypass would reduce train traffic in the East End of Houston, benefiting both the public and the railroads.
Ripple effect view: shows physical track connectivity from UP's own corridor definitions. It does not simulate operations; it identifies which lines and junctions the added trains must share. Prepared July 2026. This map presents public data for civic review and is not endorsed by UP, NS, FRA or STB.
